description: Each player represents a football (soccer) club and moves a token up and down a short track, signing pro players and drawing event cards. When two players meet on the track going in opposite directions they face off in a match, the outcome of which is determined through rolling dice based on the players signed to each club. Some matches also trigger betting. Victory points are accumulated by winning or drawing matches, and the game is played to a number of points agreed on beforehand.

boardgame: SUGAR DROP (シュガードロップ)
description:
User summary
In the game of シュガードロップ (SUGAR　DROP), you are one of a group of teen girls, and your goal is to brighten your life by dressing up and getting rid of stress.

Although the game features girls, what they show is not just sugar-coated cuteness. The theme of the game is the cuteness and the slyness of girls.

They are 'frenemies' who work against one another while pretending to be friends. For example, they sometimes don't invite a certain girl to a tea party. A girl's slyness like this adds cuteness to themselves.

Each turn, you may take one action from these options:
1) Buy a dress card
2) Activate a dress effect
3) Remove a stress card from you
4) Give a stress card to an opponent
5) Suppress one of an opponent's cards
6) Recover one of your suppressed cards

You need to have a certain coordinate points to buy a dress, and a certain appeal points to remove a stress. These values are changed by some card effects.

At the end of the game, total those values and subtract the remaining stress values from the total. The highest scorer win the game.

Publisher's summary
プレイアブルキャラクターがすべて年頃の少女であるカードゲームです。
少女たちが主役となるゲームですが、彼女たちが発揮するものはお砂糖のようなかわいらしさだけではありません。
このゲームのテーマは少女たちの「かわいらしさ」そして「おそろしさ」です。
一人だけ仲間外れにするティーパーティー。
普段は仲良しの友人として振舞いつつ、影では足をひっぱるフレネミー。
みんな友達で、みんなライバル。
少女たちの少しおそろしい部分が刺激的なスパイスとなり、かえって少女たちのかわいさを引き立てます。

ドレスカードで着飾り、ストレスカードをなるべく排除し、ライバルたちよりも輝かしい自分を目指していきます。
さまざまな役割を演じるアクトレスカードを追加して、難易度を調節することも可能です。

足の引っ張り合いが主眼となるゲームですが、協力プレイモードもあるので、フレネミーな関係に疲れてしまったら、少女たちの仲良しな甘い関係もお楽しみくださいね。
designers: Nana Coconotsu (ここのつ　なな)
artists: Nana Coconotsu (ここのつ　なな)

description:
This game includes 4 dice-games with a game board to document the winning points with wooden counters (blue=100, black=10, red=1). If a counter gets on a red or white number the player gets a penality (retreat a number of spaces or pay game token).

1. Die 999
The first player to roll 999 or higher wins the game. Each player rolls 3 dice. The lowest number is multipled by 100, the next highest number by 10. If a sequence is rolled (1-2-3, 2-3-4, 3-4-5, 4-5-6)the player pays 2 token into the playing till. By the roll of a triplet he has to pay 2 token the player to his right. If he rolls "Kanone" (2-1-x, 3-1-x, 4-2-x)he has to pay 2 token to the player to his left. In all 3 cases, the numbers don't count for winning points.

2. Die lustige Sieben (The funny seven)
The Players rol 3 dice. The first to reach 999 wins the game.
Rolls that include "7" (all 3 or 2 dice) or "14" (all 3 dice) get special points. There is a rise- and a descent-phase. During the rise the player who rolls the lowest number gets the Points of the player who rolls the highest number and vice versa. During the descent it's the opposite.

3. Hunderteins gewinnt (101 wins)
This game is played with 1 die. The player to exactly reach 101 wins the game. If a player rolls over 101, the surplus points are substracted. Each turn the player may roll as often as he choses. But if he risks to much and rolls a 1, he only gets  1 point for the turn.

4. Max & Moritz
This is a combination of game 1 and 2.

boardgame: Lightning and Steel: The Battle of Piotrków Trybunalski, September 4-5, 1939
description:
The German attack on Poland, codenamed “Case White,” on September 2nd, 1939 was a gamble on Hitler’s part. He wagered Poland’s allies of France and Great Britain would once again cave-in to his demands and not oppose his invasion. Even if they did, a lightning-fast war (blitzkrieg) would make mote any of their objections once the rival to the east was subjugated. That a mutual defense treaty stipulating an invasion from the east by the Soviet Union only “sealed the deal” and further secured Hitler’s risk.

Lightning and Steel is a war game simulation of the climatic battle fought near the town of Piotrków Trybunalski during the 1939 invasion of Poland by Nazi Germany. Taking the crossroads town was crucial to the German’s XVI Corps offensive. The Poles ordered their 1st and 2nd Tank Battalions, along with elements of two infantry and one cavalry divisions, to counter attack and cut off the Germans. The result was the war’s first major tank battle, and one that nearly thwarted the German’s plans. Can you do as well or better than you historical counterpart? Learn and enjoy!

Each copy of Lightning and Steel is composed of the following:

Two 11 by 17 inch hex map sheets
One sheet of 108 double-sided, unmounted counters
One Players' Aid Sheet
One 7 page set of rules

From the box

"Biblical Buddies draws both child and adult alike into a land where 20th century meets biblical history. Experience the exciting journey from the Garden of Eden to the Promised Land. Meet Adam and Eve, Noah, Moses, David and Goliath and all the Biblical Buddies characters. Players begin in the Garden of Eden and embark upon an adventure through major events of biblical history including Noah's Ark, David and Goliath, the Parting of the Red Sea and many more. This journey includes both setbacks and rewards with the ultimate goal of reaching the Promised Land."

Biblical Buddies is a roll-and-move game, with each player working towards reaching the end of the track first.  Many spaces on the board have instructions to either help you move forward faster or to go back, lose a turn, etc. As players traverse the board they will read about many different biblical characters. A provided information booklet allows the player to find out more about each character and the role they played in the biblical stories.

boardgame: Mystic Vale: Vale of the Wild
description:
Description from the publisher:

Vale of the Wild adds a large variety of new advancement and vale cards to the base Mystic Vale game. Players can start the game with new leader cards in their deck. Leaders grant abilities that can be upgraded to become even more powerful! New eclipse advancements may be covered by another advancement and still add its ability to the card, offering greater flexibility to craft cards and enhance powerful combos.

description:
"Sangoku!" is a card game for two people in which the players become monarchs of one country, led by warlords, aiming at unification of the three countries, set in the world of the Chinese Three Kingdoms period.

Each player draws cards from the deck alternately one card at a time and place cards in his hand next to one of the 5 castle cards placed in the field. You can declare "Siege" by arranging 5 cards next to the castle. You can earn a castle if your opponent does not have five cards at his next turn in the siege declaration. However, in case he managed to place five cards in his side of the castle sieged, a decisive battle will be held to win the castle by the stronger combination of cards. A player who wins 3 castles first wins the game.
publishers: Little Future (リトルフューチャー)

description:
The discovery of Pearl Lands, a galaxy with abundant resources, changed the face of the space race forever. Fleets traveled to Pearl Lands to transport new red shiny Pearl resource to our solar system, an adventure that was not possible before the use of Pearl extensively in every part of space technology. And the gift of Pearl Lands was not limited to Pearl! A green rain made of grain, discovered on a Pearl Lands’ planet, created a new energy source for space technologies, and a curious little yellow flower with beautiful petals provided Mana, a seed to be cultivated with utmost care. So, with these three new resources previously unknown to mankind, space travel to the farthest points of the Pearl Lands galaxy became a reality, and with the establishment of colony after colony Pearl Lands has become a new home for mankind.

Of course, war has never ended. With the use of new technologies, it just became more sophisticated. Fights for planets turned into a fight for resources, whether by using fire power or trade channels. Prosperity was never enough for mankind. There was always more to gain, and more to develop. Now it's your turn to join the race. Trade, fight and build future technologies. Power is a beautiful thing, so make sure you have it all…

Pearl Lands is a 2-4 player resource management strategy game with a sci-fi theme in which you compete to build three main resource buildings before other players. It’s a fast paced game that gets more and more intense with each passing turn.

The game has different strategy elements like attacking opponents to steal their resources, trading resources and using universe cards to change the course of the game (sometimes just slightly and sometimes drastically). Its combat mechanics is similar to Risk, while trade aspect gives it more like a Catan style strategy element. However there is no tile or troop movement factor that complicate things: it’s designed to be fun and straightforward.

Pearl Lands is full of surprises: the game can turn even at the last round. However it’s not a game of pure luck. The player with long term winning strategy will definitely have the upper hand, and most likely will be the target of other players banding together against her. Alliances are of the utmost importance. Cunning players will find the game to their liking, but utilizing only the art of deception may not be enough to win the game. Sometimes desperate measures like a kamikaze attack to destroy five resource crystals by sacrificing an attack ship can change the balance of power. And buying universe cards can pay off greatly if you get one extra attack or defense die, or get to change the resource setup on the board to sabotage an opponent's game plan.

description:
Multiverse: Cosmic Conquest is a game for 2 or more players which combines traditional collectible card game mechanics with a game board (galaxy) on which the game is played. 
The contents of the galaxy are different every time and are unknown to the players at the beginning of the game. Players slowly explore the galaxy during gameplay. 
In Multiverse: Cosmic Conquest players
Over the course of the game, each player will take turns playing cards such as units, structures, actions, and reactions, engage in battles, explore spaces in the galaxy and discover and colonize planets. Each player starts the game on their home planet. When you conquer your opponent’s home planet or control 10 spaces in the galaxy, you win the game!

Convoy Commander is a Nautical World War 2 themed Solitaire dice placement Card-Wargame using cards to determine events during each day of the convoy. Your convoy has 6 Destroyers, an Escort Carrier (CVE), and an older Battleship. You play the Convoy Escort Commander, in charge of the Convoy's escort, attempting to keep your Merchant Ships safe by eliminating threats during a series of separate attacks.

This game is being prepared for the 2016-7 Wargame Print and Play Contest.

description:
Description from the publisher:

In Dungeons Are Dangerous!, players will use their best excuses to avoid another adventure in a treacherous dungeon because...dungeons ARE DANGEROUS!

Description from the publisher:

In Letter Tycoon: Captains of Industry, players mix and match new rules with those in the Letter Tycoon base game, including global tycoon powers, grand skyscrapers and challenging regulations.

A new promo Djinn for Five Tribes from Days of Wonder, offered first as a reward in some Kickstarter and Indiegogo campaigns.

Each time a player other than you ends their move on a market tile, you draw the first card from the resource deck.

boardgame: Core Worlds: Alternate Heroes Promo Cards
description:
A set of 6 cards that were never released to the public. They were available only through the Core Worlds Digital KS in 2015.

Core Worlds: Alternate Heroes Promo Cards provides fans of Core Worlds with a new look at the Starting Heroes, giving the great game of Core Worlds even more game play diversity.

In Grossburgers, you are a line cook in a 1950's hamburger joint.  Your job is to serve as many burgers as you can, no matter how gross the ingredients.  In fact, depending on the customer, the grosser the better.

Grossburgers is a real time dice rolling game about building and selling burgers.  Players, at the same time, roll their dice trying to get sets of icons to do different actions in the game.  When one player has an action that requires them to ring the bell, everyone must pause play and let them perform their action.  Then that player rings the bell and everyone can start 
rolling their dice and performing actions again.

Players will fulfill customer orders with ingredients ranging from classic to grossout!  Ingredients on burgers are worth points at the end of the game but watch out for grossouts because they will cause you to score negative points.

Grossburgers is a fast and frenzied game of burger building madness!

Teddy Hedz, or THZ for short, is a card game in which you attempt to hit your opponent repeatedly with a range of soft plush toy animal heads. The victor is rewarded with the right to hit their opponent with a real plush toy animal head, adding humiliation to failure.

Each round has a build phase, a battle phase and a draw phase. In the build phase players set up defenses, boost their abilities or initiate a battle. If a battle is declared, all players participate in the battle phase; pitting the skills of their THZ, as well as a variety of Buffs, Environments, Interrupts and Bearicades, in a duel. Winner scores virtual SCONs against their opponent. At the end of the battle, all players draw a card.

The first player to reach 10 SCONs — or the player with the most SCONs when the cards run out — wins and may claim the THZ to hurl at their opponent.

No actual teddy bears were harmed in the making of Teddy Hedz.

One Drunk Night... is a dark humor story-building game where players craft a drunken adventure together.  The result is unpredictable and frequently crude with potential catastrophic moral repercussions on your psyche.

In this game, the Storyteller begins by choosing one of two "Beginning..." cards to start the adventure.  The other players choose one "...Middle..." or "...End" cards from their hands to continue the story and play them face down.  The submitted cards are shuffled and read aloud by the Storyteller.  The Storyteller then chooses the card he/she wants to use to continue the story.

The player who submitted the chosen card receives one point if a "...Middle..." card was selected and two points for an "...End" card.  Play continues with the same Storyteller until an "...End" card is selected, adding to the last selected card each time to grow the adventure.  After an "...End" card is chosen the adventure is read aloud and play rotates to the left.  Stories can go as long as the players decide allowing for much more replayability and variety than play X match Y mechanic found in other party card games.

Players decide the ending conditions and the player with the most points is the winner if score is being kept.

description:
Rules:
The player holding this card may, at the end of phase C, move one of his cubes to the palace area if at least one other player’s cube is killed by the Plague. The cube must be taken from a region adjacent to the region where the rat tokens are revealed (it doesn’t matter whether this region contains rat tokens or not).

End of the game: The player may move the Plague Piece one step in the final counterclockwise round. If the Plague kills another player’s cube in this region he can save one of his own cubes from a neighboring region.

Card combinations: If a player also holds a card depending on plague piece (Chivalry characters or Serf), he must apply the effects of these cards in the same region.

Cities of Splendor is a quartet of expansions for use with the Splendor base game. Each expansion is added to the basic game and they should be played separately.

The Cities replaces the noble tiles with 3 different city tiles (randomly taken from a pool of 14). The city tiles are objectives (in prestige points and/or development cards) and you need to fulfill one of them in order to win.

The Trading Posts are special bonuses you earn by acquiring an array of development cards: more prestige points from the 1st noble tile you receive, an extra gem of a different colour when you choose the "Take 2 gem tokens of the same color" action, and so on.

The Orient adds three decks of cards (one for each level of development cards). They are added on the right side of the regular cards and you place two of them face-up on the table for each level. The new cards have special powers (like double bonus cards or joker cards which take the color of one of the developments you already own).

The Strongholds expansion gives each player three towers (strongholds). When you acquire a new card, you must put a stronghold on an face-up card on the table. You're now the only player able to purchase/reserve it. You may also move one of your strongholds from one card to another one or remove another player's stronghold. When your three strongholds are on the same card, you can buy it after your regular action, allowing you to make two acquisitions in the same turn or buying that card after taking your tokens!

boardgame: Perdition's Mouth: Abyssal Rift – Hero Trait Deck
description: Optional cards that add more personality to the heroes along with unique benefits for each. A card for every hero we’ve designed.

This mini-expansion includes:

- Mogba’gh Miniature
- Hero deck (13 cards)
- Hero sheet
- 2 shield markers
- 2 stickers for shield markers
One extra wound card
One caged victim miniature

Everyone knows that the Big Four want control over Mars to boost their profits. But what can Mars offer that’s profitable enough to justify the enormous expenses of terraforming a barren, irradiated desert? What’s valuable enough to warrant shipping supplies across the solar system and fighting with the entrenched Clans?

On Mars, as the newest Agendas for each faction demonstrate that HB, Jinteki, NBN, and Weyland can take their goals much farther than they can on Earth. Away from the regulations of national governments and the incessant monitoring of citizen journalists, these corporations are able to pursue their most extreme ideas with impunity.

And now, as the Big Four are growing confident enough about their power over the Red Planet to pursue their wildest agendas, it's time for the Clans to choose their path. Will they band together to oppose the MCA or will they let old feuds tear them apart? The corporations say that they can bring water to Mars, but whose blood will pay for it?

The sixty new cards (three copies each of twenty different cards) of Blood and Water reveal the corporations’ plans for Mars and give the Clans, along with their netrunning allies, a chance to stop them
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable
family: Collectible: Living Card Game (Fantasy Flight Games)

boardgame: Genso Suikoden Card Stories Trading Card Game
description:
Description from the publisher:

Genso Suikoden Card Stories is a collectible trading card game based on the Japanese role playing video game series, Genso Suikoden. The card game used characters encompassing virtually all of the main numbered titles in the video games with the exception of Suikoden V which did not appear in the cards at all. All of the cards as well as the rules are in Japanese only as the game has never been made available outside of Japan.

The game is a trading card game in which players draw cards from their respective decks and take turns playing cards onto the field. Each player uses a deck containing 50 cards. Before the game begins the players agree on a set number of VP (Victory Points) needed to win the game, the rule book suggesting 5 VP being the standard amount to win. Much like many other card games the game can also be won if the player's opponent cannot draw any more cards from their deck.

Players prepare their 50 card deck before the game and once prepared and turn order established, begin the game. Player's place Mission cards and attempt to fulfill the card's qualifications in order to acquire the VP gifted by the card, as marked on the card itself. The opposing player can also attempt to fulfill the cards qualifications, winning the VP for themselves.

Besides this, players can also attempt to construct Facilities cards. They are built in much the same way as Mission cards are played but do not provide VP. If the player succeeds in building the Facilities card they played, they gain the bonus granted by that card. If the opposing player wins, however, the Facilities card is considered to have been destroyed and no bonus is granted to either player. Character cards are used to either attack mission cards to earn the VP or to either build or destroy facilities depending on who played the Facilities card.

Play continues in this manner, with control passing between each player at the end of each turn until one player meets one of the victory conditions listed above.

Description from the designer:

What it is:
Killer Dice is a quick and easy dice rolling game for two or more people that plays in less than 10 minutes.

What you get:
Killer Dice comes with 6 dice of different colors, 2 sets of dice labels (in case things get messy), 5 red damage counters, directions, and a drawstring bag to hold all your goodies together.

What you do:
Each player takes a single turn of five rolls of the included dice. After each roll, the rolling player may choose to use one rolled special ability to affect the roll results or damage the Killer Die. They then remove any eliminated victim dice from the game. The player then rolls again until they either complete five rolls, run out of victim dice, or chase off the Killer Die by dealing at least 5 damage.

How you win:
After each player has taken their turn of five rolls, the player with the most surviving victim dice is declared the winner. In case of a tie, the player who also dealt the most damage to the Killer Die wins. If no player has any surviving dice, the Killer Die wins.

Description from the publisher:

The young sorcerer's apprentices have to set forth alone through the dark enchanted forest for their big test. Fortunately clues about the right path appear in the magic ball — but the sorcerer’s apprentices have to be quick and look very closely because the player who can conjure up the clues will move ahead one step closer to their destination. The player who first finds the path to the sorcerer's secret room is the quickest to pass the sorcerer's exam.

In more detail, in 1, 2, 3 – Hex Herbei! the young magicians must be the first to reach the end of the path, and to advance their figure to the next stage of the path, they must spot one of the items on that path in the crystal ball that lies in front of them. Each round, all players grab the crystal ball in front of them, searching to find one of the four items before anyone else can do the same. When a player finds an item, they advance on the board, then all players change seats! Now you're looking for something new as each crystal ball contains only one of the four items shown on each section of the path, which means that you're looking for something new almost every round.
Slide your pieces across the game board in Skatter and hope to land them in the right holes to score.

To set up Flip'n Chips, pour all of the chips onto the table, then lay them flat. Players take turns flipping chips over to match colors and shapes. Get seven of your matching chips face up and you win!

It has been a year since the five tribes of Stratos began their journey - each carving their own path to prosperity in the fabled land of legend. Plentiful resources have begun appearing along fault lines almost as if they were gifts from beings dwelling in the depths below. The people thought the gifts a blessing but they could not have been more wrong...

Stratos: Light in the Darkness plays as both a themed extension to the original 'Stratos' and as a stand-alone 2-player game. Exciting new challenges await, including Monsters which add a co-operative element to the game. When combined with the original, Stratos: Light in the Darkness can be played with up to 7 players!

Stratos: Light in the Darkness has 5 character classes, each serving a distinct function. Characters move from space to space on a tile-based board which can be arranged randomly or according to set maps. Each character can perform up to 2 unique actions per turn with movement being a repeatable action. Available character actions (dependent on class) include Harvest, Travel, Search, Attack, Shoot, Defend, Cast and Use.

Points are awarded by defeating characters, casting unique spells, discovering treasures, hoarding resources, promoting characters, occupying strategic locations and slaying Monsters! The first player to reach a pre-determined number of Prosperity points (default 10) is declared the winner.

Stratos: Light in the Darkness is a product of Board and Tale Games Inc., a Canadian boardgame publisher based in Hamilton, Ontario.

Whim is a risky word game in which location is everything. Players race to find words with letters that they choose, and the letters must appear either at the beginning, in the middle, or at the end of the word based on the die roll that round.

In Akin, players use suction cups to suction up cards on the table that are related — a.k.a., akin — to the card revealed at the start of the round. The first person to suction up two related cards wins the round, and whoever has won the most rounds after ten rounds wins the game.

boardgame: A Defiant Stand: What If the Japanese had landed at Midway
description:
What if the Japanese had landed at Midway?

A Defiant Stand  is a hypothetical game exploring what might have happened if the Japanese won the naval battle of Midway and carried out their plans to invade the islands.

Ziplock game, includes 11*17 mapsheet with rules, and 5/8" counters.

Players will need a standard deck of playing cards and one six-sided die to play the game.

Also includes a small expansion scenario for the TPS game The Battle of Midway 1942 AD (1 page of color rules and additional counters)

Description from the publisher:

In Derelict, you are a Space Marine detailed to protect a science team. This team has discovered an artifact orbiting Taurus III, a distant planet located millions of light years from Earth. Initially, there was no sign of anything wrong.

The artifact changed that.

You watch, horrified, as the scientists you swore to protect quickly turn into the stuff of nightmares. The infected crewmembers grow massive scythe-like claws where their arms used to be, razor sharp fangs in place of teeth, and horns now protrude through the skin all over their tortured bodies. These poor souls now only have a singular mission: kill anything in their path.

As the last healthy human aboard, you need to focus on surviving long enough to get the hell out of here!

Derelict is a game for 2-5 players divided into two asymmetrical teams. One player is the human, tasked with restoring the derelict space ship to working order and escaping an untimely end. The other team plays as the infested, who make up the remainder of the players, and have only one goal: kill the human player at all costs!

A classic trick-taking, bid-making card game for contemplative players. This medieval-themed game combines elements of collusion, misdirection, risk taking and deduction. Successful players must manage the tension and uncertainty surrounding 4 Specialists, including the powerful Raven.

The game is played with 56 suited cards — Shields, Swords, Axes, and Waters numbered 1 (low) to 14 (high) and 4 special cards (specialists).  Each hand features a different trump suit.  Players must sacrifice cards from their hands to bid the number of tricks they will take.  Each suit conveys a bid value:  0, 1, 2, or 3.  The suit (not the rank) of the cards chosen determines how many tricks the player must take. Score 1 point per trick taken, plus a bonus for an exact bid.

The specialists are:  

        Raven —  the highest trump
        Wolf — the highest non-trump
        Shaman — a half-step higher than the card it immediately follows
        Dwarf — the lowest card


Each player tries to take (win) the exact number of tricks bid.  Deception and collusion are allowed and include saying:  how many tricks you bid; what cards you hold; that you want a particular trick; that you won’t take a trick; and lying about any or all of these things.  You can say what you like, but you cannot show your cards before you play them.

Card play mechanics are typical for the trick-taking family of games: The player left of the dealer leads.  Play proceeds clockwise.  A player who can follow suit must either (1) follow suit or (2) play a specialist. A player who cannot follow suit may play any card.

The player with the most points after 8 hands wins. In case of a tie, the player (of those tied) who earned the most bonuses is the winner.

Simple roll-and-move game of the Game of Goose variety, probably sold in the 1940s or 1950s. Two Dutch children find a giant clog in the attic and set out on a circular trip down a canal, using the clog as a boat. While the kids mostly want to take their time sightseeing and collecting some choice tulips, the goal for the players appears to be to return home as quickly as possible. Various spaces contain the usual instructions to take a detour or shortcut, move a number of spaces ahead or back, or lose a turn.

Description from the publisher:

description:
A quantum vortex is rearranging reality... history and fantasy are being jumbled together and somebody needs to untangle the mess! You must find the lost people and creatures then get them to follow you back to their homes. Lead enough followers to safety and you might just be able to destroy the vortex and rescue reality!

Follow is a tile/card matching game based on strategy and luck.
Players create a different board each time by joining together the hex-tiles (111mm max. width) as the game progresses. Each hex shows a different location and in each location there is a 'follower' card (45x63mm).

The locations and characters are well known from history or fantasy but are randomly mixed. The objective is to lead these famous faces to their specific places. For example; you could find a bewildered Dracula in the Wild West and lead him through Ancient Egypt and Camelot to reach his Transylvanian home.

The rules provide a choice of actions each turn, such as exploring new locations and stealing followers from other players as well as the opportunity to have several turns in a row, allowing players to plan strategies.

The followers and locations are colour-coded as yellow, green, blue, purple and red. To win the game a player has to return one follower of each colour to the safety of their specific locations. Upon achieving this feat the player must then succeed a dice roll called the 'Gizmotron roll' in order to reset reality and therefore be declared as the winner.

An eight page (A5) 'Instruction Manual' gives rules for 2 to 6 players aged 8 to adult. A standard game comprises 30 'follower' cards, 30 corresponding location hex-tiles, 6 'vortex' hex-tiles and lasts from 20 to 60 mins, depending on the number of players. The game is made quicker and easier by reducing the number of follower cards involved, along with their location hexes.

Tenth Edition featured 383 cards (121 rare, 121 uncommon, 121 common, 20 basic lands). It was the first Magic Core Set, after the Limited Edition, to be printed with all black borders. [2] It was also the first time since 5th Edition that the core set contained substantially more than 350 cards. For the first time, Wizards of the Coast put legendary creatures in a core set [3]: each color received two legendary creatures, and a legendary artifact, Legacy Weapon, was also included.

They were made available through the website, Here I Rule. "Here I Rule" was a marketing campaign to reach new MtG players. Krill is a tile-laying strategy board game where the players take turns to place tiles on the game board. As they do so, their krill swarms emerge and grow. boardgame: Forbidden: A Mature Party Game for Immature People
description:
Description from the publisher:

As we like to say, this game is not for the easily embarrassed, often offended, or humorless among you… Forbidden is a game for those of us that revel in watching our friends squirm, get flustered, and curse loudly. If this is you, then welcome to Forbidden!
 All players split into teams and in each round one player will try to get his team to guess the target word, without using any of the Forbidden words listed on the card.  If the player can get their teammates to guess the target word they score a point and move on to the next card, the object is to score as many points as possible in the time allowed.  However, if the player messes up and uses a Forbidden word or says any part of the target word then the card and the point go to the other team.  Additionally, if you are caught using any of the Forbidden words then you must draw a Forbidden card, these can be beneficial, but usually they are an additional obstacle that you must deal with on your next turn. 
 We also have four types of special cards included in the deck-
 Skip- Allows you to skip past a difficult card without a penalty or without the other team getting a point.
 Steal- This card allows your team to jump in for a round and try to guess the target words while the other team gives clues.  Any words that you guess first will score a point for your team.
 F@*K You- Use this card to pass the most challenging cards to the other team, they must start their turn with that card.
 Wild- This card works as any one of the other cards, your choice!
 That’s it, that’s all you need to know to play the game, simple right?  However every game is unique based on who you play with and with a massive deck of Forbidden cards to play through you should expect countless hours of laughing at your friends.  
 Forbidden- A mature game, for immature people

boardgame: The Quick and the Dead: Coastal Actions in WWII
description:
Description from the publisher:

The Quick and the Dead (Q&D) is meant to replicate the sharp, short actions involving coastal forces during World War II. More specifically, this set of rules and cards is focused on the fighting between coastal forces in the Mediterranean Sea, but if the rules prove popular, expansions for the English Channel and the Pacific theater are planned. It is important to recognize, however, that these rules focus on only a part of the operation of coastal forces. Coastal actions are characterized by patrolling and stealth, usually at night, punctuated by a quick, violent engagement at close quarters, often less than 500 yards.

Quick and the Dead features a unique diceless and turnless system in which players delcare and play action cards as fast as they can.  This creates an unusual tunnel vision phenomenon in which players are focused on the actions of their small craft and can be surprised when an enemy comes alongside and opens fire.  Games usually last between 30 and 45 minutes, so you can play two or three in an evening.

PT-boat actions are generally divided into three phases. There is the stealthy approach phase in which one force lies in wait, trying to get an advantageous position on the enemy. This phase can last several hours, beginning from the time the boats leave port until (and if) contact is made with the enemy. There is the assault phase, in which the boats battle each other, typically at fewer than 200 yards, and nearly always at night. This phase usually lasts between 20 and 40 minutes. Finally, there is the aftermath. Typically one side or the other affects a disengagement and both sides head from home.

The Quick and the Dead focuses on the middle phase. Games last about 40 minutes. There are no turns and no chart cards. Players use cards to control the movement and firing of their boats. All the needed information is on the cards. When a player has exhausted his cards, each of his boats makes a morale check, draws more cards, and continues playing. At the end of a game, players feel exhausted, as if they've just been through a fight.

If you like your naval games pedantic and chart-ridden, The Quick and the Dead is not for you. If you want to feel as if you've actually been in a PT fight, these are the only rules that give you that feeling. The game plays very different than most game you've played. Some players get the hang of the fluid, no-turns system quickly. Others have difficulty. I have found that by a player's third game, they get the hang of it. Many don't like the game the first time they play, but after playing a couple more times, really enjoy it. During play tests some have suggested that I should include an optional standard-style turn sequence. While players would be free to do so, I've decided not to include a tradtional turn sequence, because then The Quick and the Dead would be just another game, and I wanted it to be unique.

The Quick and the Dead comes with a rulebook, the cards to play the game, scenario cards, and boat record cards for most of the German, Italian, British, and American boat types in the Mediterranean. I usually play on a 4'x6' table, but that's for a six-player game. You could certainly play on a dining room table. The basic set of cards is for that size game. The advantage of the .pdf format is that if you want to player larger games (more players) you can print another deck of cards.

From the back cover
Wellington Rules is a set of rules designed to fight tactical engagements during the Napoleonic Wars. Wellington Rules is designed so that each player would control approximately a brigade of troops.  
In Wellington Rules players command brigades and divisions in small tactical actions of the Napoleonic Wars. 
Players will find that Wellington Rules makes the command of these elements challenging and fun while the streamlined, consistent mechanics makes the game easy to learn and simple to play. With Wellington Rules you can concentrate on tactics, not charts.

Scales: 
1 infantry or cavalry figure = 30 men,
1 cannon casting = 2 guns,
1 gunner figure = 10 men,
1 inch = 15 yards,
1 turn = 15 minutes

boardgame: Big Battles for Little Hands: Wargaming Source Book for Children
description:
Description from the back of the book:

This book is an introduction to the hobby of playing tabletop battles with miniatures. This a fun, interesting and educational pastime for children of all ages. This book traces its roots from games played by H.G. Wells and R.L. Stevenson in their backyards around the turn of the century.
Big Battles is meant to be a sourcebook, a great deal of information has been packed into it. Big Battles for Little Hands includes:

An introduction to wargamming

A brief history of land warfare

A primer on tactics

A complete set of rules for playing battles: Milk and Cookie Rules

A complete set of rules for playing skirmishes: Blood and Swash

Scenarios

Guides on where to find model soldiers

An introductory guide on how to paint soldiers

A guide for parents

and much more...

Beer and Pretzels Urban Combat (B.A.P.U.C.) is a simple, fast-playing skirmish war game using modern small arms in a hypothetical future of civilian urban warfare, using a card based turn sequence.  Players agree on the size of each army made up of urban guerillas (typical scenario calls for any guerilla troop of five to eleven guerillas, though enough tokens are available to field 18 guerillas per side). Players also agree on weapon equipment ranging from pistols to machine guns and grenade launchers.  Using a shuffled deck of standard playing cards, turns are determined by the draw of the cards – red cards allow the red force to move, while black cards allow the blue force to move.
Combat is based on the individual guerilla with a health of four points each.
Optional rules include Personalized Guerilla leaders, each with their own special rules.
Six scenarios are provided with the game, each with their own victory condition and rules for the scenario.  One of the scenarios is designed for four players, while the largest scenario (22 guerillas in combat) plays in about 30 minutes.

Rules are only five pages long and includes double-sides reference chart for weapons and movement effects, two additional pages of optional rules, a diagram illustrating valid attacks, and a sheet containing the six scenarios. The game comes with a preprinted hex-based game map and an uncut token sheet for fire effects and guerillas for both red and blue forces.

Eliminate the opponent's pieces by mastering a shrinking and transforming playing area, and tiles that fix the type of movement of the pieces.

In turn, each player moves one of his pawns, then removes from play the tile his pawn was placed on. If the removed tile creates an empty space in the playing area, the player moves either side of the row or column to form an uninterrupted row or column. If this reconstruction creates an empty space again, he can move either side of the row or column again and so on. The capture of an opposing pawn is done by moving to the tile it occupies. The winner is the player who captures all 4 opposing pawns.

Contains:

36 tiles,
    8 wooden pawns,
    1 rule booklet.

boardgame: The Battle of the River Plate
description:
The Battle of the River Plate is a Tactical level board-wargame of the December 13, 1939, engagement between the German cruiser Admiral Graf Spee and its British pursuers, the Ajax, Achilles, and Exeter. Components consist of a rules booklet, a Combat Card, four ship-armor diagram cards, plastic blocks labeled as the ships, and blocks in several colors use to designate hits.

The game is intended to be played on a hex board supplied by the owner. The rules specify use of an Avalon Hill Stalingrad board so that starting hexes can be specified in terms of Stalingrad locations. Pieces use both hexes and hexsides for movement to double the number of possible facing directions.

Movement is plotted and executed simultaneously. Rules cover gunfire, torpedoes, smokescreens, and ammunition supply. There is no time limit; play continues until the Graf Spee escapes or is sunk.

The stated purpose of the game is to "introduce the designers method of naval combat". One year later Bruce Moore published the better known game Early Pacific Battles.

boardgame: Monopoly Junior: Eloise's Rawther Exciting Globetrotting Adventure
description:
Description from the back of the box:

Join Eloise, Weenie, and Skipperdee on a globetrotting adventure around the world. You'll visit Eloise's favorite cities - Paris, Moscow, and of course New York, and learn lots of interesting facts about landmarks and famous places.

Along the way you can buy famous properties, put up penthouses and of course say "Charge It Please" when someone comes to visit.

The player who collects the most money at the end of the trip wins.
family: Versions & Editions: Versions with IP after the original release

description:
Recreation of a soccer game.
Teams are formed by 11 players.
Rules are simple. 
Game for 2 players.
Field is divided into square areas.
There is a maximun number of players allowed per area.
Passes, driblings and shots to goal are checked by a die roll.
In advanced game there are action points for movement, an for dribbling.

A multicolored collecting game for 2-4 players.

Each caterpillar has a headpart and a tailpart.
Shuffle up the 6 different colored bodyparts.
Roll the die and match the color of the visible bodypart.
If it match, add the bodypart to your caterpillar.
Or wait untill next turn.

Who can complete his caterpillar, with 6 different bodyparts, first and wins the game?

boardgame: The Battle of Midway 1942 AD: Independence Day
description:
Independence Day Scenario : the following  variant rules, and the accompanying counters, posit the Japanese launched their offensive a month later (July 3rd through 5th). Both sides would've been able to reinforce the forces used here, but would they have made a difference? Play the game with variants and find out!

Bonus scenario offered by Turning Point Simulation (for TPS's subscribers)

A promotional card given out during the 2017 The Game Boy Geek Kickstarter campaign.

This promo introduces a new Encounter Card (#39) that you can use in the game. The cards shows some mechs wandering into a meadow with cows and an electric line.

The card offers the following options:

     Steer the mechs away the electric lines: Gain 2 food and 1 popularity.
     Get awkward cow-milking lessons from the farmers: Pay $2 to gain 4 food.
     Siphon electricity to turbocharge your armament: Pay 2 popularity to gain 5 power.

description:
From the box:

From the Deep is a Lobotomy Board Game Expansion Set inspired by the dark and twisted novels of H.P. Lovecraft.

There is an ancient evil lurking in the basement of the mental hospital. The Deep Ones, an amphibious humanoid race, are preparing a ritual to call upon their deity. The only people aware of the danger are the patients of the asylum. Are they crazy or is Dagon the Great One really coming to destroy reality?

Find out with 5 game changing modules included in the expansion. Go deep with new miniatures: a new character Philip Howard, a horde of Deep Ones and the terrifying Father Dagon. Alter the gameplay with a set of small boards - the locations of challenging rituals. And finally get rewarded for saving the world by a deck of mysterious, powerful, beautifully drawn artifacts.

boardgame: Flames of War: Nachtjager – The Battle for Northern Germany March-May 1945
description:
Description from the back of the book:

This is one of the Late War Supplements for Flames of War. It is focused on the British attacks on Germany late March 1945 onward. It includes histories of the battles for the northern Rhine including Operation Plunder.  Game options include rules to field a British Rifle Company, Armoured Squadron and the B Squadron, 15th/19th Hussars. Also are rules for fielding a Marine-Grenadier Kompanie, Panzerkampfgruppe Wallenberg and Panzerkampfgruppe von Benninsen.
This supplement features optional rules for fighting battles at night.

description:
User summary
PLANESS is a territory occupation game with a theme of space exploration.

There is a virtual square grid on the table, and each player places their space ship on the grid at the beginning of the game.

Each turn, your space ship moves several squares on the grid. For each square you passed, if it is unoccupied by any player, you place your planet chip on the square to indicate that this planet is yours. You may add mores chip on your planet by passing the same square again.

You may also pass an opponent's planet. In this case, the topmost chip on the square is removed, and if this make the square empty, you place your planet chip to indicate you have taken over the control of the planet.

Players count their victory points at the end of the game. You score big points if you have a large contiguous area of your planets. The highest scorer wins the game.

Publisher's summary
惑星探索をテーマにした陣取りゲーム。
ゲーム終了時に最も得点が高いプレイヤーの勝利。
自分の惑星を確保しつつ、相手の惑星を奪い、破壊することが勝利の鍵。
チーム戦も可能。
publishers: Board Game Parfait (ボードゲームパフェ)

description:
Based on an online word game, 'Text Twist' challenges players to make as many words as possible out of a given set of letters within a 2 minute period.

A game consists of 5 rounds.  During each round, a card is drawn which lists a series of letters.  Information is given on the card detailing how many possible 3,4,5,6 and 7 letter words can be created from that specific mix of letters.  Players write the letters on their individual dry erase board, then the timer is flipped and players have 2 minutes to produce as many words as possible.  At the end of the 2 minutes, players score points for valid words created (a booklet is included listing all possible words from each combination of letters).   The score for that round is recorded, and the next round begins with a new set of letters.  After 5 rounds, the player with the highest score wins.

The game can be played as a solo puzzle challenge.

Contents:
4 wipe-off Answer/Score boards
1 Instruction/Solutions booklet
144 cards (864 challenges!)
4 dry-erase markers
1 2-minute timer

boardgame: Stock Trader: The Stock Market Board Game
description:
Your rich uncle just gave you some investments.  Can you make some smart moves and turn them into a fortune?

Stock Trader is a simple and fun stock market game that you can play solo or with the whole family.  Do you have what it takes to be a smart investor on the stock exchange?  Find out with Stock Trader, The Stock Market Board Game!

The objective of the game is to become the richest player by buying and selling stocks and gold.  Buy low, and sell high!

The main board tracks the value of each investment.  The value of each investment is increased or decreased by rolling dice each turn, if the positive die (green) is higher than the negative die (red), the value moves up that many spaces.  If the red die is greater than the green die, the value drops and could even crash that stock completely!  Each turn there is a new 'Headline News' card that can affect the stock values in addition to the dice rolls.

After the dice are rolled and the values set, each player decides what they would like to buy and sell and tracks their shares on their own 'Portfolio' card.

After 10-turns, everyone sells their investments and the player with the most money wins the game!

A Co-operative Game with an emergency-room simulation theme.

Gameplay is cooperative. Players are all on the same team and take turns being team leader. Players take decisions on who to admit to an emergency room bed, how to distribute limited resources of skilled personnel to address injuries. Game mechanisms control the flow of patients, nurses, doctors and time. Beds are limited, injuries vary from critical to nominal. Patients have varied age range and varied gender/appearence.

The goal is to treat as many patients in one 8-hour shift as possible. Players do not win or lose, but treating all 15 patients is a perfect game, leaving some patients for the next shift is to be avoided if possible.

Japanese children's 'wargame'. 1-4 players move from their starting spaces (2 as submarines, 2 as bombers) by die rolls, directed through a network of spaces by the number they roll, scoring points for the enemy ships they land on and 'sink'. With luck, one player can reach the victory space in the minimum of 5 moves.

The spaces in descending order are:
Victory:  
大戦果    Dai Senka / Big Battlefield
60 points:
レキシントン USS Lexington (scuttled 8/5/1942, Battle of the Coral Sea)
プリンスオブウェールズ HMS Prince of Wales (sunk 10/12/1941, off Malaya)
25 points:
甲巡 heavy cruiser
甲巡エクゼター HMS Exeter (sunk 27/2/1942, Battle of Java Sea)
甲巡 heavy cruiser
15 points:
乙巡 light cruiser
乙巡トロンプ型 HNLMS Tromp (severely damaged, 19-20/2/1942, Battle of Bandung Strait)
乙巡デロイテル HNLMS De Ruyter (sunk 27/2/1942, Battle of Java Sea)
乙巡ジャバ型 HNLMS Java (sunk 27/2/1942, Battle of Java Sea)
15 points:
特巡 merchant raider
乙巡 light cruiser
輸送船 transport ship

My Booty! is a speed and dexterity game for 2 to 4 players.
Each round, a set of lookout cards is revealed, then players must try to be the first to push forward the appropriate pirate tokens in front of them and slap on the treasure chest tile to score points.

Different lookout cards call for different pirate tokens. Act quickly, but don't twist your brain!
Be the best and fastest pirate on the sea and enjoy some chaotic fun!

City Development game

game-play
Each turn draw a map tile, and place it with others map tile(s). If the map tiles form a 2/3 circle, you can draw a extra map tile from supply. 
Then for each circle you form, you can gain 1 building tiles. 
You can change city cards by building tiles.

goal
Be the first player collect 3 city cards.

boardgame: British vs Pirates: Volume 1 – Spanish Armada Add-on
description:
Full faction add-on that adds the Spanish Armada to the core game of British Vs Pirates.
Originally this faction came with 25 Skill Cards, but after reaching a Social Goal the number was increased to 27 Skill Cards.

This add-on features:


     18 D6 dice (purple)


Ship miniatures (yellow):


     2 Sloop
     1 Fluyt
     1 Zebec
     2 Frigate
     3 Galleon


Additional miniatures (yellow):


     1 Fort


Cards:


     27 Skill Cards
     9 Ship Cards
     5 Captain Cards


Tokens:


     6 Crew Tokens

This add-on adds reinforcements for both the British and Pirates factions with more ships and captain cards.

Includes:

     2 Pirate Ship Cards
     2 Pirate Ship Miniatures
     1 Pirate Captain Card



     2 British Ship Cards
     2 British Ship Miniatures
     1 British Captain Card



     12 D6 Dice

In 8 Out Of 10 Cats, players or teams take turns answering questions on opinion polls, surveys and statistics.

There are five different categories of questions, including "Poll with a Hole", "And the Winner Is ...", and "Pick of the Polls". Players roll a die in order to determine which kind of question they have to answer. Once a player or team has correctly answered ten questions, they have to successfully answer an "8 Out Of 10 Stats" card to win (and end) the game.

Join the dynamic action embedded in the turbulent times of World War II. The heroic struggle, full of sacrifices and tough decisions will make even you fail to notice how much you consume climate unpredictable clashes on the battlefield. Take on the role of an all-powerful commander and lead your troops on the way to victory.

Autarcha is an innovative strategy, which compete with each other two parties to the conflict. Rich in variants of tactical gameplay makes every time we experience a meeting with the game again. Enhanced combat system and countless possibilities are distinguishing features of the product. Trust us and let yourself be drawn into a new world of Autarcha.

The game takes place on the board, on which are marked various landscape elements: land, forest, mountains, rivers and cities. The board is perforated by transport links, such as road and rail. Each of these elements can be used in the game: Rail and road allow faster transport, forest and mountains hide the army and the cause that they are difficult to destroy, and the water stops, eg. Armor or artillery, which they can not overcome these obstacles. It depends on the player how to use elements of the board.

Most important is the general staff - in most scenarios, the destruction ensures victory. It represents the player and can give orders to the other four types of units:

Infantry - cheap unit with a relatively small range. Easy to recruit and rapid implementation to fight.
Sappers - a specialized unit performing special operations, eg. Putting min, which in turn are invisible to the enemy
Panzer - unit with a high cost, but high mobility in the field and long range.
Artillery - a unit of positional far, code coverage.
Branches can attack at a distance in their moves during the turn.

The game is included economic aspect, each player can build infantry and sappers in the city and armored units and artillery in our factory.

The game can be started in a simple way - choose the same set of units for each player, set them on the board and start the fight.

In Hollywood Boulevard, players are theater owners tasked with picking films that will generate more money than any of their competitors. Each turn represents one week wherein films will be played. Each film will generate money by rolling dice. The more dice, usually the more money. There are various methods of gaining more dice for the films. Advertising, amenities, and chance cards will add to the amount of dice rolled.

This is a dexterity game with an unusual box. It is hinged on one side, and when opens, creates an extended lithographed playing field. The bottom of the box has a ramp starting at the hinge, and five scoring slots on the far side (worth 10, 20 and 50 points).

Players take turns shooting marbles from the top end, over the ski jump and (hopefully) into one of the scoring slots. Player with the highest total score for six marbles wins.

This game was produced by the German publisher Klee, but the box says "Made in Bavaria" and bears the company's early "Clover" international logo (Klee is German for Clover). While undated, this certainly means it was published after Moritz Bromeisl joined the company in 1915 and began moving it into international markets, and probably between the end of WWI and the early 1920s.

Description from the publisher:

A shark vs manatee? No contest. A shark with a pool cue vs manatee with a bazooka? Interesting... A couple of billion years ago, evolution (or whatever) invented animals. A couple of million years ago our pre-human ancestors invented weapons. A couple of minutes ago we gave the former access to the latter.

Animals with Weapons is a casual card game for 2-4 players. It develops the idea of the traditional trump-card game by incorporating two main decks (Animals and Weapons), along with a 'sub-deck' of Terrain cards, which are combined to make animal/weapon combos. This eliminates much of the repetition of traditional trump-card games and allows strong weapons to compensate for weak animals. A 'capture or loot' mechanic also removes the stalemate which so often develops in trump-card games.

132 cards, with 60 animals in each of the main decks, makes for 3600 combinations in the base game, with multiple game modes to broaden gameplay.

Designed to be played in under half an hour, AWW is quick, brutal and very silly.

This all-wood game was published in Canada in 1943, clearly cashing in on the popularity of the British wartime Prime Minister. The solid wood board shows a circle with 4 concentric rings divided by 10 radii. There is a centre space marked with a Crown, and each of the 41 resulting spaces has a hole into which a wooden peg can be placed. There is a hole in the side of the board stopped with a cork in which 20 pegs are stored.

Each player starts with 10 pegs, placed in the outer two rings of half the circle. The pegs start with a plain side up, and are marked with the player's colour (red or black) on the other end.

Players move alternately, either clockwise, counter-clockwise or toward the centre. As in normal checkers, they must jump over an opponent into a vacant space if able to do so. A piece moving or jumping into the Crown space at the centre flips into a King, which is then allowed to move and jump outward as well as inward. A piece in the Crown space is safe, and only a King can jump back into the Crown space and continue jumping.

Normally, a player wins be eliminating the opponent's pieces, but there also are rules for a "losing" game where the objective is to give away all your pieces or create a situation where you are unable to move.

While the rules show the publisher as London Novelty and Display Products, the box says it was manufactured by Robinson Industrial - Crafts Limited, also of London, Canada.

This is a French journalism-themed card game in which players try to collect sets of reporters, stories and documents. There are three types of cards: 18 Reporter cards (2 each of an English, French and German reporter with corresponding camera and transport cards); 18 Story cards (color-coded red, blue and orange, each with 2 Event cards, 2 Place cards and 2 Interviewee cards); and 18 Document cards (red, blue and orange, each numbered 1-6).

Players collect combinations of sets, each of which can be a Reporter with matching equipment, the Event, Place and Interviewee of a color, or three consecutive Document cards of the same color.

Each player starts with 2,700 in tokens and a hand of 8 cards, and each turn draws a card and discards a card. The other players in turn order have an opportunity to buy the discard if they can use it to complete a set of three, paying 20 into the pot if they do so. Other players may overbid if eligible, paying 40, 60, etc. If the discard is not claimed, play passes to the left; if it is claimed, play passes to the claimer's left.

A hand is won when a player completes his hand with one of 7 combinations of sets. The winner takes the pot plus a score that varies with the difficulty of the combination, from 500 for two series of Reporters and one of Stories to 5,000 for three series of different Reporters. Winner of the game is first to 20,000 points.

The game box features celebrated French journalist Georges de Caunes. The artwork suggests it dates to the 1960s.

16 Promo Cards for Click Clack Lumberjack, which add an additional level of fun and surprise to the game!

Each player is given a random card at the start of the game, and card gives a one time use special action which alters the way that either they or the opposing player(s) must play that round - examples include; force a player to play with their non-dominant hand, force an opponent to play a stroke with their eyes closed, instantly replace a core you knocked off

This is a compilation of 12 sports games. Some of them were available separately as part of the "ZDF-Sportstudio-Linie".
The games included are:

     Schwimmen (tile-placement-game)
     Zehnkampf (board game)
     Schießen (tactical board game)
     Boxen (card game)
     Radrennen (board game)
     Reiten (board game)
     Olympia*Quiz 
     Handball(boardgame)
     Leichtathletik (dice game)
     Tennis (board game)
     Hockey (board game)
     Fußball (board game)

In ancient times, sorcerers divided nature into 5
elements: Air, Fire, Water, Earth and Lightning.
It has been said that these five elements are the
Building Blocks of Life.

Goal of the game : To collect the most element points in your spell book.

You have one visible preferred element and one secret preferred element and 5 to 12 cards in your hand (from 5 to 2 players).

you play elements cards from your hand on the table to create a Block of Life (BoL: one card of each element) or you swap your visible or secret element with another player (and draw a card).

When you create a BoL, you pick one for your spell book and discard the rest.

The game ends automatically when one of
the following situations occur a player collects a Block of Life (a set of 5 different elements) in his spell book or specific situations suche as a player empties his hand or the drawing pile becomes empty.

boardgame: Masmorra: Dungeons of Arcadia – Arcadia Crossover Kit
description:
The Arcadia Crossover Kit includes Hero Boards and Hero Cards to bring all of the Heroes from the two Arcadia Quest core boxes and two expansions to your games of Masmorra. That's a 28 Heroes, which means 28 Hero Boards and 56 Hero Cards are included in the Arcadia Crossover Kit.

The Arcadia Crossover Kit does not contain figures, if you want to properly use these Heroes, you will need to have the Arcadia Quest core box or expansion that contains their figures. Here's the complete list of the Heroes contained in this kit, and what Arcadia Quest box they come from:

Arcadia Quest:
Grom
Scarlet
Greensleeves
Johan
Maya
Seth
Kanga
Spike

Wisp

Hobsbawm

Arcadia Quest - Inferno:
Bjor
Cassius
Diana
Izzy
Jack Jack
Jaimie
Kisha
Mark
Natasha
Phyx
Sybbyl
Trandir

Arcadia Quest - Beyond the Grave:
Chaz
Darryn

Arcadia Quest - Pets:
Greenhood
Oak

Unleash spectacular combos and devastating finishing moves in this jaw-dropping, butt-kicking fighting game of martial arts mash-ups! For 2-4 players.

In Samurai Punk, players assume the role of deadly warriors in an original world that blends steampunk with martial arts - samurais, ninjas, kung-fu monks, gangsters, gunslingers, knights templar, and more! Combining a Fighter deck and a Sensei deck, players create a unique fighting duo to duel against their opponents.

Combos are formed by pairing a sensei's Style cards with a fighter's Strike cards, creating one of thousands of different moves, like "Flying Kick", "Fierce Defensive Slash", and "Ultra Spinning Dragon Uppercut". Each round, new Styles or Strikes are played, replacing some cards in the players' Combos while retaining the others. Used cards store up Chi energy which can activate special abilities, making every card count. Master your duo's unique strategies and versatile combos to out-fight your opponents to victory!
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
There are 3 game variations, all on the same game board. Two players Play with 12 playing pieces each.

A. Einkreisungsspiel Maja
One playing Piece of each player is called the "Maja". Which one is hidden from the Opponent (marked on the bottom). The players try to encircle the enemy pieces. Encircled pieces are taken out of the game. If the black Maja is encircled on a yellow space it may reenter the game, but if it ist encircled on a blue space the opponent has won the game. The opposite is true for the white Maja.

B. Rösselpunktspiel
The pieces start at one edge of the game board and have to reach the opposite edge. The pieces move like a chess knight. The first player to get all 12 pieces to the opposite side of the game board wins the game.

C. Türmerspiel
The pieces start at one edge of the game board and have to reach the opposite edge to turn into towers. The pieces move any number of spaces in all directions. Opposite pieces can be taken by skipping over the piece sideways to a free space directly behind it.
Pieces that reach the opposite edge of the game board, get turned into a tower by stacking a second piece on top of it. Towers may take enemy pieces (or Towers) by skipping over them not only to the space directly behind the taken piece but to several spaces behind, if there are no other pieces or towers in-between. The last player to have any playing pieces on the board wins the game.

A simple yet engaging, tactical trench warfare card game themed around the misery and glory, the hope and despair of one the most historic battles in human history i.e. World War I - Battle of the Somme. It features a patently new and unique COVER AND REQUISITION wargaming system that allows for:

1. 2 player fast paced action
2. Tactical turn-based strategy
3. Grinding trench warfare
4. Simple game mechanics
5. Easy to learn
6. Quick setup
7. Challenging gameplay
8. Suitability for ages 10+
9. Short 15 minutes playtime
10. and much more!

Somme: The Story

The year is 1916. A great war rages across the world. “The war to end all wars” some call it and it seems to be living up to its name, for a more terrible yet strange war the mankind had not seen before.

It is definitely nothing like those that came before it, for here the men lie, waiting, almost peacefully, in the lines they have drawn. For months together, nothing has stirred over the now, black land. No bird flies over, nor any beast roams here. The silence is almost unbearable, as the men eat, sleep and go about their business in absolute monotony.

Suddenly, the frail sense of security is shattered by a deafening noise, as a bombshell explodes nearby, causing the men to drop their jobs and scuttle for cover. One explosion is followed by another and another and another… and so the pounding goes on and on… for days and days… till all nerves are frayed and every muscle is jittery. Just when they were beginning to think that it would never stop, it ends, as abruptly, as it had started.

Deafened by the onslaught on their ears, the soldiers strain to hear, fearful of that which is to follow. Soon enough, they hear a distant pattering of feet, getting closer and closer or are they? Surely, it is the sound of gunfire that they hear now or are their ears still playing tricks on them? The men arise as if from a great slumber and peer over the parapet. What they see is incredulous, for what was earlier a barren wasteland is now a rising sea before them. Their knuckles whiten as they tighten their grips over their weapons and wait for the incoming tide to draw closer, nay, wash over them.

It is clear, that thousands will die this day and whether or not they themselves would survive, will ultimately depend on your wisdom, as their commander and the support of the brothers, by their sides. So take charge and stand fast. For the empire!

Somme: The game

Setup: To play, each player chooses and commandeers a faction, the British or the German, consisting of a squad of 6 soldiers that are laid out in a couple of trench lines on the table. From among the rest of the shuffled cards, 20 cards are passed between the players and 10 are drafted by each player. Of these 6, are assigned to the soldiers already on the table while the rest 4 are held in the player’s hand as faction reserve. 2 cards are opened at the draw pile to form the common supply line.

Strategy: Each of the fighting units under the player’s command has a different loadout and a susceptibility to specific hardship cards. Further, the unit loadouts keep changing over the game and may also be affected by unveiling of certain event cards. The crux of the game's strategy however lies in bidding from the supply line which will ultimately decide the actions available in the future.

Objective: The object of the game is simple, eliminate the rival faction before they eliminate you.

Turn Mechanics: The play alternates between the players as they play cards from their reserve to the Personnel Cards in the trench lines seeking to form compatible sets of Attack-Defence Cards in order to launch attacks, counter-attacks or to defend. Dictated by the ensuing card play outcomes, the soldiers may either loose morale, gain morale or be eliminated. At the end of each turn, both the players may bid to draft the available replacement cards from the common supply line as they prepare to face another sapping day in the trenches. Thus, the grinding battle continues, till only one side remains standing.

The penitentiary is in chaos. Prisoners are on the loose and rioting, and it’s your job to restore order. To make matters more difficult, the inmates are grouped into seven factions, and you have to round up as many of these gang members as you can to have any hope of success.

Lockdown! is a streamlined, themed version of the card game Canasta, specifically designed for two players. The 64-card deck consists of 49 Gang cards and 15 Special cards. Major improvements include simplified scoring and opportunities for cunning card play.

One of the problems with Canasta is that the player (or team) which takes the inevitable, massive discard pile will usually win the round. In Lockdown!, discard piles are far more manageable, and you can even remove up to two of them from the game! (Don't worry, there's a counter to this.) Additionally, four cards will score a meld (as opposed to seven in Canasta), and of course there are incentives to go higher than four.

Lockdown! plays quickly, and most of the rules are on the cards for easy reference. First player to 100 wins the game!

PoPalotsy is a speedy dice game in which players try to be the first to get rid of all the cards in their hand.

Players are each dealt 5 ring shaped cards - each card depicting a die face on a colored background.  When all players are ready, 5 different colored dice are popped out of the provided popper.  Players then race to encircle the dice with their cards by matching the color of the die or the number on the die. A number match beats a color match and an exact (color AND number) match beats all.  The first player to drop all of their cards wins.

Contents:
Popper
5 Dice
40 Ring Cards 
Rule Book

The Pups Promo Pack includes 5 new cards:

The Legendary Pups and the Underdog card can be added to any game of Pups for a new play experience!

The 4 Legendary Pups are: Cerberus, Argos, Laika and Saurr, and they respectively take the highest seat of power (10) for each suit.

The Underdog acts as a special catch-up card awarded to whatever player trails behind most in the game.

Each Pups Promo Pack also includes a rules card.

Blend it like Becca has introduced their summer menu: the Mystery Twisters. For a limited time, pick your favorite fruit smoothie, and Becca will add a secret "twist."

The Mystery Twisters are added to the deck per the normal rules: always add the orange card, and then add whatever colors you are playing. Unlike the Surprise Me card, there is one restriction to the Mystery Twisters: the 4th fruit must be different than the other 3, and the durian does not count!

Sticko is a game by David Parlett, designed to be a solitaire game of his similar game Stucco. In this game, you are trying to build up a sequence of cards based on these three resrictions:

You can only play a higher rank of the same suit, a lower rank of the opposite suit of the same colour, and the same rank of either of the opposite colour suits.

Labirynt Śmierci (Labyrinth of Death) is a cooperative game in which players explore big and very unusual maze - this is not a typical underground, every single location may contain large areas, a big pack of monsters or even entire castle. You can meet the essence of both very bad and very good. Often, instead of fighting, you can make a deal and sometimes to gain allies...

Each player controls a group of one to three characters. These are the heroes and even gods or beasts like dragons, known from world of RPG game Kryształy Czasu (The crystals of time).
Each character is represented by two or three profession cards with different stats and skills (one of them is choosed during every encounter) and five to seven cards with spells and special powers. This are one use only with some chance to take it back.

At the beginning of the game, players choose the type of expedition and how many encounters they want to play. Single encounter involves drawing a single location (there are 250 of them), after which the players choose which profession will have their characters in this challenge. Then they find out what is happening and who they face. If there is a fight, players must collect with their parameters, spells and abilities enough Power points and Trap points to reach danger lever in this location. If they do it they acquire treasures, if they don't they receive wounds.
After all the encounters is checked whether players were able to meet the objectives related to the expedition they choose.

boardgame: Test of Honour: The Samurai Miniatures Game – Ronin
description:
The masterless samurai known as ‘ronin’ roam the land as mercenaries and highwaymen. The warband of Tadashi ‘the Dishonoured’ has a reputation for using bribes, dirty tricks and even poison to massacre its enemies.

Ronin have often been presented as noble figures, forced to make impossible choices while seeking revenge for the death of a beloved master. As such this set contains a range of Infamy skills which take your samurai far away from what was considered honourable behaviour.

However, just as often they ended up as mercenaries, thieves and thugs, abandoning their code for the sake of survival – so we’ve included Recruitment cards to use these models as commoner Ronin if you wish, giving you basic troops with a slightly different stat line.

Contains:

18 unique cards - including Hidden Attacker and Dangerous Reputation
    1 metal character model - Tadashi ‘the Dishonoured’
    5 plastic figures
    5 metal 'scruffy' heads
    2 mulltibases

boardgame: Test of Honour: The Samurai Miniatures Game – Pauper Soldiers
description:
Disinherited samurai lord Senzo ‘the Thorn’ leads a group of pauper soldiers, using his legendary skills with the bow in the hope of winning back his fortune.

Once again, the metal straw hat heads that come in this set completely change the feel of the models, making them less military and more like peasants (especially if you leave off the back-banners).

Remember each expansion set contains one type of plastic sprue, but there’s nothing to stop you mixing the heads around on bodies from other sets if you want to create a more balanced force.

The samurai archer character from this set is backed up with some useful shooty skills, plus other skills and quests that reflect him being more at home with the local peasants than most noble-born samurai.

Contains:

18 unique cards - including One with the Target and Hidden Marksman
    1 metal character model - Senzo ‘the Thorn’
    10 plastic figures
    10 metal 'straw hat' heads
    3 mulltibases
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

boardgame: Test of Honour: The Samurai Miniatures Game – Masked Men
description:
Warrior Monk Masahiro has vowed to fight the injustices of the Japanese clans. He and his followers battle the power of the samurai, taking the armoured face-plates of their fallen enemies as badges of victory.

The stealing face-plates idea has no historical grounding, but we thought it was a cool idea that might well have happened. Perhaps more importantly it demonstrates what a different look you can create by changing the heads.

Warrior Monks meanwhile are an established part of the history of the period and had many conflicts with the ruling samurai clans. Like the other new metal character models Masahiro has his own character card (costing an extra point or two to recruit), or you can simply use one of the standard samurai cards (yes, even though monks weren’t samurai!).

boardgame: Test of Honour: The Samurai Miniatures Game – Mounted Samurai
description:
Following the murder of her husband, the widow Takeda Naoko dons the armour of a samurai and rides out to seek vengeance, all the while clinging desperately to the path of honour.

The rules for cavalry in the game are very simple (presented on the recruitment cards in this set), but adding even just one or two to your force changes things dramatically. The extra movement multiplied by 3 actions for your hero means you can get your most powerful model exactly where you need him (or her). And an all cavalry force is very interesting to play!

The onna bugeisha character (female samurai) comes with skill cards to show her sense of honour, family pride, and a haughty attitude to the low-born, plus a particularly interesting quest where you have to interrogate enemy samurai once they are vanquished to discover a secret. The idea is that she is tracking down her husband’s murderer. Could it be the ronin Tadashi?

Contains:

18 unique cards - including The Side of Right and Powerful Charge
    1 metal character model - Onna Bugeisha Takeda Naoko
    5 plastic mounted figures
    5 metal 'neat bare' heads
